topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom felt thick, suffocatingly sweet with expensive perfume and forced merriment. I watched from the periphery, nursing a lukewarm champagne flute, my gaze snagging on him across the crowded floor. Liam. He looked… polished. Too polished. He was laughing easily with the groom’s parents, his arm draped casually around a woman who clearly adored him. A tight knot formed low in my stomach, a familiar, unwelcome clenching. Every time his eyes flicked in my direction, I felt a distinct hardening around my jaw. I forced myself to adjust my jacket, the silk feeling suddenly abrasive against my skin. I wanted to disappear, to melt into the decorative floral arrangements. When he finally turned, catching my eye for a fraction of a second, there was a flicker—a brief, almost imperceptible acknowledgment—and then he looked away, the smile tightening just a little too much. I took a slow, deliberate sip of my drink, letting the silence between us stretch, heavy and charged.

[story 2]

“Oh, look who decided to grace us with their presence,” I muttered under my breath, loud enough only for myself to hear. My shoulders were hunched, a defensive posture I couldn't seem to shake. Seeing him there, standing near the buffet table, looking impossibly happy, sent a sharp, unpleasant jolt through me. It was like watching a perfectly executed performance I wasn't invited to attend. I kept my hands firmly clasped around my glass, the crystal cool against my palms. When he finally spotted me, there was a momentary pause in his conversation, a subtle shift in his expression that made my chest tighten. I deliberately turned my attention to the canapés, focusing intensely on the miniature quiches, trying to appear utterly absorbed in the trivialities of catering. The way he offered a quick, polite nod—a gesture devoid of warmth—was enough to confirm everything I already suspected about the distance between us.

[story 3]

I leaned against a marble pillar, trying to anchor myself to the cool stone, anything to stop the internal tremor that had started in my hands. The music pulsed around me, a relentless, cheerful wave that seemed determined to erase any sense of quiet misery I was cultivating. Then I saw him. He was talking animatedly, his gestures broad and open, radiating a kind of effortless connection that felt utterly alien to my current state. A distinct sourness rose in my throat. I watched him move, cataloging every casual interaction, every shared glance with the wedding party. When our paths momentarily intersected near the dance floor, I kept my expression carefully neutral, a mask of polite indifference stretched taut over my features. I offered a brief, almost curt nod, ensuring my eyes met only for the necessary second, before turning my back sharply, needing distance more than I needed the validation of a shared moment.
